questions.question: What does the 60/40 rule mean for AI-assisted acquisition?

options.text: 60% of budget on tools, 40% on content
60% of time on high-judgment human activities, 40% on AI-assisted execution

questions.explanation: The 60/40 rule means spending 60% of your acquisition time on high-judgment human activities (calls, creative outreach, relationship building) and 40% on AI-assisted execution (research, drafting, data work, follow-ups). Going 100% automated produces mediocre 2-5% reply rates; the hybrid approach achieves 8-15%.

questions.question: Which day in the weekly rhythm has the highest human-to-AI ratio (94% human / 6% AI)?
questions.explanation: Thursday's discovery calls and warm outreach block is 94% human / 6% AI. This is the highest-leverage hour of the week because reading between the lines, building rapport, and qualifying prospects in real-time are skills AI cannot replicate. The lesson emphasizes protecting this block ruthlessly.

questions.question: What is the recommended total weekly time investment for sustainable AI-assisted acquisition?
questions.explanation: The lesson recommends 5-7 hours per week distributed across five sessions: Monday research (90 min), Tuesday drafting (60 min), Wednesday follow-up (45 min), Thursday calls (90 min), and Friday metrics (45 min). This is sustainable long-term and produces 3-5 conversations per week.

questions.question: The 'batch processing' approach — doing all research in one session, all drafting in another — is less efficient than handling each prospect end-to-end one at a time.
questions.explanation: False. Batch processing is significantly more efficient because it eliminates context switching, which makes each task take 2-3x longer. The batch pattern (research 20 prospects in one session, draft 20 emails in another, review all 20 together) is one of AI's key efficiency multipliers.

questions.question: Design your personal weekly acquisition rhythm. For each of the five sessions (Monday-Friday), specify the exact time block, target output, and tools you will use. Explain how you will match high-judgment activities to your peak energy hours.
questions.aiPrompt: Evaluate whether the response includes all five daily sessions with specific time blocks (not just 'morning'), concrete target outputs (not just 'do research'), and tool selections. Look for energy-task matching — are discovery calls scheduled during peak hours? Is data work during lower-energy windows?
